title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear an art restorer called Michael Reed talking about his work on famous paintings. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear an art restorer called Michael Reed talking about his work on famous paintings. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Michael Reed explains the delicate process of cleaning and repairing centuries-old masterpieces.
extracts.audio_script: Good morning. My name is Michael Reed and I’ve spent the last twenty years working in the restoration department of the National Gallery. People often imagine us working with magnifying glasses and tiny brushes, and while that's true, modern restoration is a high-tech science. [7]I actually started my career as a specialist in seventeenth-century oil paintings[/7], but I now work on works from almost every era. The first step in any project is analysis. [8]We often use ultraviolet light to identify areas of a painting that have been repaired in the past[/8]. It’s like looking at a map of the work's history. Cleaning is the most nerve-wracking part. [9]I found that using a new micro-suction tool was much safer for removing dust[/9] than traditional methods, as it doesn't touch the paint surface. Sometimes, we have to remove layers of old varnish that have turned yellow. [10]We always apply a thin layer of natural resin to protect the painting once the cleaning is finished[/10]. It restores the original depth of the colors. Working in the lab can be physically difficult. [11]I was surprised to find that the constant chemical smells were the hardest thing to get used to[/11], even with our advanced ventilation. It’s a very clinical environment. One of my favorite projects involved a landscape that had been hidden under a portrait for 200 years. [12]Maintaining total historical accuracy during that restoration was a huge responsibility[/12]. You don't want to add anything of your own. Looking forward, the field is changing. [13]I believe that digital archiving is becoming an essential part of the restoration process[/13]. It allows us to keep a perfect record of the work before we even touch it. As we often say to students, [14]our job is to be an 'invisible bridge' between the artist's original vision and the modern viewer[/14]. If you can't see our work, we’ve done a good job.

dialogue.speaker: michael
dialogue.text: Good morning. My name is Michael Reed and I’ve spent the last twenty years working in the restoration department of the National Gallery. People often imagine us working with magnifying glasses and tiny brushes, and while that's true, modern restoration is a high-tech science. I actually started my career as a specialist in seventeenth-century oil paintings, but I now work on works from almost every era. The first step in any project is analysis. We often use ultraviolet light to identify areas of a painting that have been repaired in the past. It’s like looking at a map of the work's history. Cleaning is the most nerve-wracking part. I found that using a new micro-suction tool was much safer for removing dust than traditional methods, as it doesn't touch the paint surface. Sometimes, we have to remove layers of old varnish that have turned yellow. We always apply a thin layer of natural resin to protect the painting once the cleaning is finished. It restores the original depth of the colors. Working in the lab can be physically difficult. I was surprised to find that the constant chemical smells were the hardest thing to get used to, even with our advanced ventilation. It’s a very clinical environment. One of my favorite projects involved a landscape that had been hidden under a portrait for 200 years. Maintaining total historical accuracy during that restoration was a huge responsibility. You don't want to add anything of your own. Looking forward, the field is changing. I believe that digital archiving is becoming an essential part of the restoration process. It allows us to keep a perfect record of the work before we even touch it. As we often say to students, our job is to be an 'invisible bridge' between the artist's original vision and the modern viewer. If you can't see our work, we’ve done a good job.

questions.question: Michael began his professional life working primarily on (7) .
questions.explanation: He mentions he started as a specialist in seventeenth-century oil paintings.

questions.question: Restorers use (8) to see where previous work has been done on a masterpiece.
questions.explanation: He says they use ultraviolet light to identify areas repaired in the past.

questions.question: Michael prefers using a (9) because it prevents any physical contact with the paint.
questions.explanation: He found that using a micro-suction tool was safer for removing dust.

questions.question: After cleaning, a coating of (10) is used to preserve the artwork.
questions.explanation: They apply a thin layer of natural resin to protect the painting.

questions.question: Michael admits that he struggled to adapt to the (11) in the restoration laboratory.
questions.explanation: The constant chemical smells were the hardest thing to get used to.

questions.question: Michael felt that (12) was the most important factor in his work on a rediscovered landscape.
questions.explanation: He mentions maintaining historical accuracy was a huge responsibility.

questions.question: According to Michael, (13) is an increasingly vital tool for modern restorers.
questions.explanation: He believes digital archiving is becoming an essential part of the process.

questions.question: Michael describes the role of a restorer as acting like an (14) for the audience.
questions.explanation: He says their job is to be an 'invisible bridge' for the viewer.
